title: National Law Enforcement Officers Remembrance, Support and Community Outreach Act.
short_title: National Law Enforcement Officers Remembrance, Support and Community Outreach Act.
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Federal Lands.
plain_summary: This bill gives money to the National Law Enforcement Museum to run special programs. The museum will get six million dollars each year for seven years from the government. They will use this money to teach people about police work, help keep police officers safe and healthy, and honor police officers who died while doing their jobs. The museum has to write reports each year to tell Congress how they spent the money.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the National Law Enforcement Officers Remembrance, Support and Community Outreach Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has determined the following facts. The National Law Enforcement Officers Memorial and the National Law Enforcement Museum in Washington DC are the only law enforcement campus in the United States. They serve an important national purpose by honoring police officers who died while doing their jobs, teaching the public about law enforcement's role in our democracy, and working to reduce deaths and injuries of officers on duty. It is important for our country to keep and improve the special education and outreach programs of the National Law Enforcement Museum. In nineteen eighty four, Congress and President Ronald Reagan approved a law that allowed the Law Enforcement Officers Memorial Fund to build a National Law Enforcement Heroes Memorial in Washington DC. The National Law Enforcement Officers Memorial was dedicated on October fifteenth, nineteen ninety one. It honors federal, tribal, state, and local law enforcement officers who died protecting our nation and its people. The Memorial is located on E Street in Washington DC and is our nation's monument to police officers who died on duty. It was built entirely with private money. In two thousand, Congress and President William J Clinton signed the National Law Enforcement Museum Act. This law allowed the National Law Enforcement Officers Memorial Fund to build and run the National Law Enforcement Museum on federal land in Washington DC to honor police officers in the United States. Construction of the National Law Enforcement Museum began in April two thousand sixteen in Washington DC across from the National Law Enforcement Officers Memorial in Judiciary Square. The Museum was completed and opened in October two thousand eighteen. No federal money was used to build the Museum. The National Law Enforcement Museum's mission is to honor America's law enforcement officers, serve as a bridge between law enforcement's past and present and between officers and the public they serve, and increase public understanding and support for law enforcement while promoting officer safety. The Museum's programs play a key role in teaching the public about law enforcement's importance in a democratic society, their role in protecting and serving the public, and the sacrifices officers make. The community education and outreach programs, activities, and special exhibits help bring local police departments closer to their communities and help create better understanding between law enforcement and communities. The Museum's Officer Safety and Wellness programs help provide local, state and federal law enforcement agencies with important information on best practices to reduce officer deaths and injuries on duty. They also provide communities and the public with education about officer sacrifices. These programs are very important when annual deaths of officers on duty remain at historically high levels. Since the Memorial was established in nineteen ninety one and the Museum was dedicated in two thousand eighteen, the Memorial Fund and Museum have worked with local, state and federal law enforcement agencies to properly honor officer sacrifices, provide key programming to reduce law enforcement deaths and injuries, and foster better understanding between law enforcement and communities. Law enforcement agencies around the country are dedicated to continuous improvement and innovation in officer and community safety, and to building trust through community engagement. Since two thousand twenty, there has been a significant increase in the number of law enforcement officers resigning or retiring. According to the Police Executive Research Forum, over the past three years there has been almost a twenty four percent increase in officers retiring and more than a forty seven percent increase in officers resigning. Many law enforcement agencies across the country remain understaffed, and many agencies have been forced to declare personnel emergencies and require mandatory overtime. The resulting lack of confidence and support puts public safety at risk. There is a critical need to enhance public understanding and appreciation of law enforcement, and to improve the safety and wellness of officers who serve our communities. Creating a more informed and positive relationship between law enforcement and the public is essential for societal safety and unity. A sustained national effort to restore confidence and understanding in law enforcement is urgently needed.

sections.title: Section 3 Support for the National Law Enforcement Museum
sections.text: During the first seven budget years that begin after this law is passed, the Secretary of the Interior must give a grant to the National Law Enforcement Officers Memorial Fund. This grant will pay for expenses related to operating and improving the community outreach, public education, and officer safety and wellness programs of the National Law Enforcement Museum. The programs include those that memorialize law enforcement heroes who die in the line of duty and compile statistics on law enforcement deaths and injuries. They honor and commemorate the extraordinary service and sacrifice of America's law enforcement officers. The programs develop and make available accurate, relevant, and accessible resources to promote understanding of law enforcement history and officer safety and wellness training, including digital resources and other types like print resources and traveling exhibitions. They increase technical resources to better engage the public in person and through online platforms to educate the public about community policing and officer safety and wellness. The programs create, expand and spread scholarly work through research, curricula, in house and traveling exhibitions, publications, and other outreach efforts. They expand the collection acquisition and collection processes, including staffing, conservation, processing, and digitization. The programs augment law enforcement history and officer safety and wellness education activities, including developing, spreading, and implementing principles of sound teaching methods for teaching about law enforcement history and officer safety and wellness. They promote professional development including local, regional, and national workshops, teacher trainings, and partnerships with appropriate organizations to better educate the public about law enforcement history and officer safety and wellness education. The programs engage with local and independent educational agencies and expand teacher engagement to cultivate and support the development of leaders in teaching law enforcement history and officer safety and wellness education. They engage with state and local education leaders to encourage the adoption of resources supported under this law into curricula across diverse subjects. The programs create, develop, implement, replicate, or take to scale entrepreneurial, evidence based, field initiated innovations for museum and officer safety and wellness improvement, and rigorously evaluate such innovations. They provide free admission to National Law Enforcement Museum for active and retired law enforcement officers and family members of fallen officers, and dedicated free admission hours for the general public at least once a week. The programs develop online Law Enforcement History and Officer Safety and Wellness Education Resources for the general public and scholarly research.

sections.title: Section 4 Progress Reports and Certifications
sections.text: For each of the first seven budget years, the National Law Enforcement Officers Memorial Fund must submit a report to the Secretary of the Interior. The report must document the progress in delivering public education, community outreach and officer safety and wellness programming, consistent with the funding provided from the Department. The report must also provide a formal accounting of total amounts of federal funds spent during the budget year. The Secretary of the Interior must share this report with Congress every budget year and make the report available on the US Department of Interior website.

sections.title: Section 5 Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: Congress authorizes six million dollars to be given to the Secretary of the Interior to carry out this section for each of the first seven budget years beginning after this law is passed. If in any budget year the amount given is not the full six million dollars, the Secretary may transfer money as needed from the National Park Service, up to six million dollars, to carry out this section.

sections.title: Section 6 Continuation of Activities
sections.text: To the extent that the National Law Enforcement Museum is engaged in an activity described on the date this law is passed, the Secretary may use the funds given under section five to continue that activity.
